title: Skipped Search Detection
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler status="skipped"
| stats count by savedsearch_name, reason
| sort -count
description: Skipped searches mean scheduled reports, alerts, and data enrichments aren't running. This creates blind spots in monitoring and compliance.
value: Skipped searches mean scheduled reports, alerts, and data enrichments aren't running. This creates blind spots in monitoring and compliance.
grandmaExplanation: Skipped searches mean scheduled reports, alerts, and data enrichments aren't running — so you always trust the numbers you are looking at.
implementation: Monitor scheduler logs for skipped searches. Alert when critical searches are skipped. Track skip reasons (concurrency, disabled, cron). Optimize skipped searches or increase search concurrency limits.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
• Ensure the following data sources are available: `_internal` (scheduler.log).
• For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Monitor scheduler logs for skipped searches. Alert when critical searches are skipped. Track skip reasons (concurrency, disabled, cron). Optimize skipped searches or increase search concurrency limits.

Step 2 — Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=scheduler status="skipped"
| stats count by savedsearch_name, reason
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Skipped Search Detection** — Skipped searches mean scheduled reports, alerts, and data enrichments aren't running. This creates blind spots in monitoring and compliance.

Documented **Data sources**: `_internal` (scheduler.log). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed—rename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: scheduler. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

• Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=scheduler. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
• `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by savedsearch_name, reason** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
• Orders rows with `sort` — combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 — Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (skipped searches with reasons), Bar chart (top skipped searches), Line chart (skip rate trend).
visualization: Table (skipped searches with reasons), Bar chart (top skipped searches), Line chart (skip rate trend).
